eason_chin.name: Eason Chin
eason_chin.summary:
Representing California, Eason Chin has tasted victory multiple times, currently competing at the U13 level, capturing 3 titles across 10 tournaments and 23 entries. Each win is earned through bracket battles against hungry opponents — and Eason keeps finding ways to come out on top.

What makes Eason especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Eason has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Yuki Guan (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Joey Wang (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
  • Allen Wu — 6 events, 1 title together in BD

Eason is peaking at the right time in 2023-2024, with 2 titles across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Eason is still ascending.

Eason has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NorCal, SoCal, Eason isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Eason brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
eason_chin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eason Chin, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Eason has 23 career entries across 10 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 13% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Eason saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Eason's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2023-2024 season has actually been the best yet — 2 titles in 5 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Eason is 0-for-10. Zero titles in 10 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Eason and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Eason needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. On the doubles front, Eason has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Allen Wu (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Anthony XIANGYU Song has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Eason's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Eason has shown up 4 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Eason has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eason has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Eason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Eason entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Eason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 23 entries, and 20 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Eason looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Eason's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Eason has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Eason. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

eason_hau.name: Eason Hau
eason_hau.summary: Eason Hau is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Eason's.
eason_hau.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eason Hau, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Eason is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC, and so far, the only thing Eason has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Eason saga began at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Eason is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Eason's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Eason is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Eason is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Eason wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Eason, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Eason, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Eason. eddie_sun.name: Eddie Sun
eddie_sun.summary:
Out of California, Eddie Sun has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 57 entries across 21 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Eddie has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Eddie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Eddie has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 13 different players across the career:
  • Ellis Ho (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (9 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Zachary Wang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (8 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Joey Wang (current partner) — 3 events, 1 title together in BD, across 2 seasons

Eddie is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Eddie is still ascending.

Eddie has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Eddie has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Eddie is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
eddie_sun.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eddie Sun, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Eddie has 57 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Eddie saga began at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Eddie's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Eddie would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 7 times Eddie has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 7 times Eddie has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Eddie's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eddie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Eddie is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Eddie and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Eddie needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Eddie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Eddie's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ellis Ho: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ellis Ho has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13 — we hope Ellis Ho has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Eddie's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Eddie has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Eddie has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Eddie is not just losing locally — Eddie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Sun family travels cross-country so Eddie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eddie has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eddie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Eddie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Eddie entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Eddie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 57 entries, and 56 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Eddie looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Eddie's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Eddie has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Eddie. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wong family travels cross-country so Eden can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eden has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eden showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13, which means Eden traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Eden entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Eden paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 36 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Eden looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Eden's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Eden has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Eden. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

edison_chen_lin.name: Edison Chen Lin
edison_chen_lin.summary:
Out of Illinois, Edison Chen Lin is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

A true triple-threat, Edison competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 15 different doubles partners over the years, Edison adapts to anyone:
  • Vedanth Menon — 2 events in BD, best: #3

Edison has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Edison brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
edison_chen_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Edison Chen Lin, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Edison has 33 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Edison saga began at 2023 Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Edison plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 33 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Edison has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Edison's texts. The go-to partner is Tianrui Peng (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U15 — we hope Beckham Chin has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Edison has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Edison has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Edison has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Edison showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Edison traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Edison entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Edison paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 33 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Edison. Edison just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Edison has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Edison. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Edison: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

edward_chang.name: Edward Chang
edward_chang.summary:
Representing New York, Edward Chang broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U17 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 28 entries across 12 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Edward is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Edward especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #4, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Edward adapts to anyone:
  • Michael Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Olivia Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Hannah Cheng (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD

Edward is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Edward is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Edward has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Edward isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Edward brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
edward_chang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Edward Chang, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 28 career entries in 12 tournaments, Edward has 1 title — a 4% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 27 entries where Edward drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 28 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Edward saga began at 2024 NE MassBad OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2026 Wayside NE CRC in XD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Edward's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Edward plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Edward is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 4th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Edward and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Edward needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Edward has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Edward's texts. The go-to partner is Ryan Chan (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Jasmine Cheung has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Edward's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Edward entered 2 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Edward's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Edward and said 'nah.'

One thing nobody can accuse Edward of is playing it safe. Edward has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Edward has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 1 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Edward has built a permanent residence on that line. Edward has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Edward has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Edward showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Edward traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Edward entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Edward paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 28 entries, and 27 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Edward looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Edward's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Edward has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Edward. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Huang family travels cross-country so Edwin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Edwin has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Edwin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Edwin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Edwin entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Edwin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 74 entries, and 68 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Edwin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Edwin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Edwin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Edwin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

eekshitha_namballa.name: Eekshitha Namballa

Eekshitha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Eekshitha adapts to anyone:
  • Ava Lee (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)
  • Kaitlyn Kim — 3 events in GD, best: #2
  • Nithya Padmanabhan — a trusted GD partner (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)

Eekshitha has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Eekshitha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
eekshitha_namballa.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eekshitha Namballa, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 25 events across 10 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 25-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Eekshitha's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Eekshitha keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Eekshitha saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eekshitha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 25 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Eekshitha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Eekshitha's texts. The go-to partner is Nithya Padmanabhan (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Nithya Padmanabhan has emotionally recovered.

Eekshitha has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eekshitha has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eekshitha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Eekshitha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Eekshitha entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Eekshitha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 25 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Eekshitha. Eekshitha just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Eekshitha has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Eekshitha. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Eekshitha: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

eesha_vikram.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eesha Vikram, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-4. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Eesha has 46 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 8 of them — a 17% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Eesha saga began at 2024 DFW South OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Eesha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 6 titles in 24 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Eesha experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Eesha has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Fortius South CRC, where Eesha entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U13, XD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Eesha does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Eesha has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 8 wins means Eesha is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eesha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 3 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Eesha can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Eesha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Eesha's texts. The most successful partnership is with Chris Lin — 12 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Chris Lin deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Chris Lin a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in GD U13 — we hope Victoria A Liu has emotionally recovered.

Of Eesha's 8 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Eesha feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Junior Nationals has seen Eesha 3 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Eesha has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Eesha is not just losing locally — Eesha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Vikram family travels cross-country so Eesha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eesha has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eesha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Eesha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Eesha entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Eesha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 46 entries, and 38 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Eesha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Eesha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Eesha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Eesha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

And to be fair, Eeshani has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Eeshani promised and what Eeshani has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 3 entries — a stretch where Eeshani actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Eeshani is 0-for-6. egbert_zhao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Egbert Zhao, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 14 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 14-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Egbert's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Egbert keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Egbert saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Egbert plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Egbert has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Egbert showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Egbert traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 SoCal CRC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Egbert entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Egbert paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Egbert keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Egbert has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Egbert, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Egbert. We will be here, taking notes.

elaine_yan.name: Elaine Yan
elaine_yan.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Elaine Yan has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Elaine's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Elaine has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Elaine competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Elaine has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 23 different players across the career:
  • Grace Zhao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5)
  • Vindhya Chekuru (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Shaoyao Luo (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Jida Wu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

The 2024-2025 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 7 tournaments — a clear sign that Elaine is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Elaine has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Elaine has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Elaine brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
elaine_yan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Elaine Yan, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 54 career entries in 18 tournaments, Elaine has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 53 entries where Elaine drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 54 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Elaine saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Elaine's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Elaine would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Elaine has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Elaine has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Elaine's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Elaine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Elaine is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Elaine has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Elaine's texts. The go-to partner is Vignan Chandrasekaran (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Jonah Chenfei Wong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Elaine's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Elaine entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Elaine's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Elaine and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Elaine has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Elaine has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Elaine is not just losing locally — Elaine is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yan family travels cross-country so Elaine can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Elaine has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Elaine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U11, which means Elaine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Elaine entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Elaine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 54 entries, and 53 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Elaine looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Elaine's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Elaine has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Elaine. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

With 18 different doubles partners over the years, Elena adapts to anyone:
  • Charvi Srivastava (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (11 total events in GD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jason Gu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Rishabh Praveen (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)

Elena has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Elena has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

elena_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Elena Zhang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-6. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Elena has 72 career entries across 25 tournaments and has managed to win 11 of them — a 15% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Elena saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in GD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Elena's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 6 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Elena actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 20 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Elena experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Elena has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Egret Midwest OLC, where Elena entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U17, XD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (5 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Elena does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Elena has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 11 wins means Elena is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Elena plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 5 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Elena can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 18 of them, to be precise. Elena has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Elena's texts. The most successful partnership is with Charvi Srivastava — 11 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Charvi Srivastava deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Charvi Srivastava a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Steven Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Elena's 11 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Elena has shown up 6 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

Elena has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Elena is not just losing locally — Elena is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 15% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Elena can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Elena has 18 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Elena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Elena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 Midwest ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Elena entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 65th. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Elena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 11 titles to the name, 72 entries, and 61 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Elena looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Elena's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Elena has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Elena. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Elena is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Elena has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Stephen Huang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Deepika Vaishnavi Venkatraman (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Leonardo Hung — a trusted XD partner (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

elena_zhong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Elena Zhong, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Elena has 19 career entries across 8 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Elena saga began at 2024 OBA NW OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Elena experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Elena has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Elena has ZERO titles. None. 3 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Elena plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Elena has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Elena has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Elena has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Elena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U13, which means Elena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Elena entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Elena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Elena keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Elena has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Elena, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Elena. We will be here, taking notes.

eli_cui.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eli Cui, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 63 career entries in 23 tournaments, Eli has 3 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 20 entries where Eli drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 63 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Eli saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in BD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Eli's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Eli experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Eli has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Eli does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Eli has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 3 wins means Eli is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Eli can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 19 of them, to be precise. Eli has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Veera Kalakota has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Eli's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Eli entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Eli's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Eli and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Eli has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Tournament committees have handed Eli a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Eli has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U15, where Eli was seeded #2 and finished 3rd. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Eli has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Eli is not just losing locally — Eli is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cui family travels cross-country so Eli can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13, which means Eli traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Eli entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Eli paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 63 entries, and 60 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Eli looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Eli's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Eli has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Eli.
