But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Eli is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eli plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Eli is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Eli and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Eli needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Eli has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Eli's texts. The go-to partner is Zelenia Shen (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Hannah Xiao has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Eli entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Eli's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Eli and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Eli 6 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Eli is not just losing locally — Eli is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The You family travels cross-country so Eli can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eli has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eli showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Eli traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Eli entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 29th, which is a polite way of saying Eli paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 49 entries, and 48 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Eli looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Eli's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Eli has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Eli. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

elina_xiao_en_yang.name: Elina Xiao-En Yang
elina_xiao_en_yang.summary:
Out of California, Elina Xiao-En Yang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-2), currently competing at the U11 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Elina's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Elina has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Elina competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Elina has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:

  • Neel Achari (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Devansh Shambu Ponnapalli (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 4 tournaments. Elina is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Elina has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Elina has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Elina's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
elina_xiao_en_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Elina Xiao-En Yang, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Elina has 27 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Elina saga began at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Elina experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Elina has reached the finals 2 times — at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Elina has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Elina plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 27 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Elina has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Elina's texts. The go-to partner is Thea Yuan (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Jeremiah Wang has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Elina has shown up 3 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Elina has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Elina is not just losing locally — Elina is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Elina can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Elina has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Elina showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11, which means Elina traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Elina entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Elina paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Elina keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Elina has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Elina, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Elina. We will be here, taking notes.

elizabeth_sarah_mark.summary:
A Texas competitor, Elizabeth Sarah Mark is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U11 level, with 8 entries across 3 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Elizabeth is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Elizabeth has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Samara Vakula Varasala (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
elizabeth_sarah_mark.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Elizabeth Sarah Mark, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 8 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Elizabeth is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Austin Leander South CRC, and so far, the only thing Elizabeth has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Elizabeth saga began at 2025 Austin Leander South CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Elizabeth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 8 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Elizabeth has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Elizabeth has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Elizabeth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U11, which means Elizabeth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Elizabeth entered 2 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Elizabeth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Elizabeth keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Elizabeth has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Elizabeth, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Elizabeth. We will be here, taking notes.

ella_kung.name: Ella Kung
ella_kung.summary:
Hailing from California, Ella Kung has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U11 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 9 entries in 3 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Ella especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Ella adapts to anyone:
  • Ethan Yichen Zhou (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

ella_lin.name: Ella Lin
ella_lin.summary:
A California competitor, Ella Lin has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (Mixed Doubles, Women's Singles) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 2 career titles across 2 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

What makes Ella especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (Mixed Doubles, Women's Singles) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U19).

Ella has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Kyle Wang — 1 events, 1 title together in XD
ella_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ella Lin, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Ella is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Ella has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Ella would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Ella, we kept reading — all 5 entries across 2 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 40% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Ella held up a trophy, there were roughly 1 other times Ella walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Ella saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ella burst onto the scene with a title in XD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Ella has delivered on some of those promises: 2 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Ella promised and what Ella has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ella experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ella has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ella entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U19, GD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ella does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Ella has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Ella is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ella plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ella can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 5 entries, and 3 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ella looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ella's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ella has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ella. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ella_mak.name: Ella Mak
ella_mak.summary:
Representing California, Ella Mak has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Ella has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Ella competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 1 doubles title, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (9 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

With 14 different doubles partners over the years, Ella adapts to anyone:
  • Yaorui Shan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (4 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Tian Qi Alan Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (3 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Nicole Krawczyk — 2 events in GD, best: #4, across 2 seasons

Ella has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Ella has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Ella is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ella_mak.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ella Mak, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 37 career entries in 13 tournaments, Ella has 3 titles — a 8% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 11 entries where Ella drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 37 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ella saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ella's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 13 entries — a stretch where Ella actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ella is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ella plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ella can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Ella has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ella's texts. The go-to partner is Yaorui Shan (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17 — we hope Devin Garg has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ella entered 9 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Ella's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ella and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Ella has shown up 3 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Ella has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ella is not just losing locally — Ella is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Mak family travels cross-country so Ella can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ella has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ella showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in GS U17, which means Ella traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Ella entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Ella paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 37 entries, and 34 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ella looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ella's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ella has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ella. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ellen_ouyang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ellen Ouyang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 11 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 11-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Ellen's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Ellen keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Ellen saga began at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Ellen has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Alfred Huang has emotionally recovered.

Ellen has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Ellen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ellen keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ellen has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. We will be here, taking notes.

ellie_chen.name: Ellie Chen
ellie_chen.summary:
Hailing from California, Ellie Chen has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Ellie's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Ellie especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Ellie has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Evan Lu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Pranav Vijay — a trusted XD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Ellie has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Ellie has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Ellie brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
ellie_chen.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ellie Chen, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 39 career entries in 13 tournaments, Ellie has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 38 entries where Ellie drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 39 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ellie saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 13th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ellie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 39 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Ellie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ellie's texts. The go-to partner is Pranav Vijay (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Zmy Gao has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ellie entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Ellie's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ellie and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Ellie has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ellie is not just losing locally — Ellie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chen family travels cross-country so Ellie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ellie has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ellie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11, which means Ellie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Ellie entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Ellie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 39 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Ellie. Ellie just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ellie has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ellie. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ellie: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

elsa_anqing_wang.name: Elsa Anqing Wang
elsa_anqing_wang.summary:
A California competitor, Elsa Anqing Wang is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U13 level, with 9 entries across 3 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Elsa especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Elsa has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
elsa_anqing_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Elsa Anqing Wang, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 9 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Elsa is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, and so far, the only thing Elsa has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Elsa saga began at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Elsa plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 9 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Elsa has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Vincent Xiaoyuan Zhang has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Elsa has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Elsa showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U11, which means Elsa traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Elsa entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Elsa paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Elsa is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Elsa wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Elsa, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Elysha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

elysha_ling.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Elysha Ling, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Elysha has 12 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Elysha saga began at 2024 OBA NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Elysha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Elysha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Elysha's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Sanjay Chittella has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Elysha has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Elysha has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Elysha has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Elysha has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Elysha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17, which means Elysha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Elysha entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 37th, which is a polite way of saying Elysha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Elysha keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Elysha has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Elysha, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Elysha. We will be here, taking notes.

emily_chan.name: Emily Chan
emily_chan.summary:
Out of California, Emily Chan has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U15 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Emily's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Emily competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Emily has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 15 different players across the career:
  • Madison Bach (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (10 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ethan Xiao-ze Yang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Emily has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Emily has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Emily has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
emily_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emily Chan, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 50 career entries in 19 tournaments, Emily has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 49 entries where Emily drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 50 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Emily saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 NW OLC in GS U11 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Emily's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Emily actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Emily is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emily plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Emily is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Emily has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Emily's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Madison Bach: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Madison Bach has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Chiranjeev Deepak has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Emily's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Emily entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Emily's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Emily and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Emily has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Emily is not just losing locally — Emily is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chan family travels cross-country so Emily can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emily has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emily showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Emily traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Emily entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Emily paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 50 entries, and 49 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Emily looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Emily's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Emily has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Emily. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

We hope Franklin Xu Cheng has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11 — we hope Jingyang Guo has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Emily has shown up 6 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5. Then there is the seeding situation. Emily has been seeded in 6 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Emily takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) in GS U13, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Emily. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Emily is not just losing locally — Emily is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Feng family travels cross-country so Emily can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emily has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emily showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, which means Emily traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Emily entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Emily paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Emily keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Emily has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Emily, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Emily. We will be here, taking notes.

emily_leung.name: Emily LEUNG
emily_leung.summary: Emily LEUNG is just getting started on the junior circuit with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Emily's.
emily_leung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emily LEUNG, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Emily is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 ABC OLC, and so far, the only thing Emily has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Emily saga began at 2024 ABC OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Emily is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Emily's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Emily is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emily has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emily showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2024 ABC OLC in GS U11, which means Emily traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Emily is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Emily wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Emily, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

emily_liu.name: Emily Liu
emily_liu.summary:
Representing California, Emily Liu is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-2 finish at Selection places Emily in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Emily is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Emily competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 4 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U17 GD, U19 GD, U17 XD) • Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U19, GD U15, XD U17) • 4 ORC titles.

Emily has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Zicheng Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/XD (5 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Reanne Chan (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Youshi Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Carryssa Bun (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3)

Emily has been on this journey through U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Emily has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Emily consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Emily is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
emily_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emily Liu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-2. Top-2 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 49 career entries in 19 tournaments, Emily has 6 titles — a 12% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 7 entries where Emily drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 49 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Emily saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Emily's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 6 titles in 19 entries — a stretch where Emily actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Emily is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Emily experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Emily has reached 8 championship matches across this career. That is 8 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 8 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NW ORC, where Emily entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Emily does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Emily has never won anything. But 8 finals losses versus 6 wins means Emily is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emily plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 4 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Emily can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Emily has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Emily's texts. The most successful partnership is with Bettie Huang — 8 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Bettie Huang deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Bettie Huang a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in GD U19 — we hope Carryssa Bun has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Emily entered 8 events with results ranging from top-2 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Emily's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Emily and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 6 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Emily has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell.

One thing nobody can accuse Emily of is playing it safe. Emily has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U15, U17, U19 — which means Emily has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 6 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Emily has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Emily is not just losing locally — Emily is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 12% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Emily can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emily has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emily showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in GS U17, which means Emily traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Emily entered 2 events with results ranging from 3rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Emily paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 49 entries, and 43 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Emily looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Emily's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Emily has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Emily. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

emily_tang.name: Emily Tang
emily_tang.summary:
Out of Virginia, Emily Tang has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U13 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Emily's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

A true triple-threat, Emily competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: ORC top-3 finisher.
emily_tang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emily Tang, from Virginia, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 3 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Emily is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 Midwest ORC, and so far, the only thing Emily has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Emily saga began at 2023 Midwest ORC with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emily plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 3 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Emily is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Emily wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Emily, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

emily_zhang.name: Emily Zhang
emily_zhang.summary:
Representing California, Emily Zhang is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 17 entries across 8 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Emily especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Emily has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Callie Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, across 2 seasons)

Emily has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Emily has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Emily brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
emily_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emily Zhang, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 17 career entries in 8 tournaments, Emily has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 16 entries where Emily drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 17 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Emily saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emily plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 17 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Emily has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Callie Chen (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GD U15 — we hope Sanika Alawani has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Emily entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Emily's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Emily and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Emily has shown up 2 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Emily is not just losing locally — Emily is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Emily can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 U.S. Selection Event. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emily has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emily showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U15 GS, which means Emily traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 U.S. Selection Event, where Emily entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Emily paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Emily keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Emily has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Emily, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Emily. We will be here, taking notes.

Emilyn is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Emilyn adapts to anyone:
  • Srinika Lingidi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Vihaan Darbha (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Emilyn has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Emilyn brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
emilyn_lizbeth_joseph.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emilyn Lizbeth Joseph, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Emilyn has 30 career entries across 13 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Emilyn saga began at 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13) with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emilyn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 30 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Emilyn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Emilyn's texts. The go-to partner is Srinika Lingidi (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Ryan Shih has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Emilyn has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Emilyn has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emilyn has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emilyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15, which means Emilyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Emilyn entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Emilyn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 30 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Emilyn. Emilyn just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Emilyn has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Emilyn. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Emilyn: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
