emma_j_wei.name: Emma J. Wei
emma_j_wei.summary:
Hailing from California, Emma J. Wei is a U.S. Junior National Champion — crowned at the biggest event on the domestic calendar in GD U19. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 3 career victories across 6 tournaments, Emma has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Emma is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals champion (GD U19) • 2 ORC titles.

Emma has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Zicheng Xu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Nicole Krawczyk (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #4)
  • Jasmine Yeung — 3 events in GD, producing 2 titles together

The 2024-2025 season has been outstanding, with 7 podium finishes across 3 tournaments. Emma is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

With tournaments spanning NorCal, SoCal, South, Emma isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Emma's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
emma_j_wei.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emma J. Wei, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won GD U19. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 18 career entries across 6 tournaments, 3 total wins, and 15 losses. A 17% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Emma has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2024 Frisco South ORC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Emma saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Emma burst onto the scene with a title in GS U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Emma has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Emma promised and what Emma has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 3 titles in 9 entries — a stretch where Emma actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Emma is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Emma experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Emma has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Emma entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U19, XD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Emma does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Emma has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 3 wins means Emma is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emma plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Emma can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Emma has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Zicheng Xu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U19 — we hope Zicheng Xu has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Emma has been seeded in 4 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Emma takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U19, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Emma. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Emma has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emma has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emma showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U19, which means Emma traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 18 entries, and 15 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Emma looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Emma's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Emma has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Emma. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

emma_liu.name: Emma Liu
emma_liu.summary:
Out of California, Emma Liu is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U11 level, with 5 entries across 3 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

Emma competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
emma_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emma Liu, from California, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Emma is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Emma has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Emma saga began at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emma has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emma showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11, which means Emma traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Arena SoCal ORC, where Emma entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Emma paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Emma keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Emma has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Emma, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Emma. We will be here, taking notes.

emma_lu.name: Emma Lu
emma_lu.summary:
Out of Illinois, Emma Lu is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the final four 4 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Notably, Emma has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Emma competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Emma has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 7 different players across the career:
  • Tianrui Peng (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Milena Sylvia Klein (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Emma has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Emma brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
emma_lu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emma Lu, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Emma has 21 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Emma saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 1 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Emma would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Emma has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Emma has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Emma's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emma plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Emma has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Milena Sylvia Klein (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Kelly Hu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Emma has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Emma has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Emma has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emma has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emma showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Emma traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Emma entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Emma paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 21 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Emma. Emma just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Emma has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Emma. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Emma: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

emma_wei.name: Emma Wei

Winning at the Selection Event (U17 GD, U17 GS) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 6 career titles across 13 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Emma is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (7 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Emma especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

  • Adam Tay (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U17/U19/XD (6 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Ben Tirtohadi — 3 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Mia Hundley — 2 events, 1 title together in GD/U17, across 2 seasons

So buckle in, because we have 21 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Emma saga began at 2023 SoCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Emma would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Emma has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Emma has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Emma's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emma plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 21 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Emma has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Katherine Zhou: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Katherine Zhou has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Daniel Li has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Emma entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Emma's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Emma and said 'nah.'

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Emma is not just losing locally — Emma is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Emma can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emma has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emma showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11, which means Emma traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Emma entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Emma paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 21 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Emma. Emma just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Emma has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Emma. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Emma: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

emma_yin.name: Emma Yin
emma_yin.summary:
Out of Illinois, Emma Yin is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U19 level, with 13 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Notably, Emma has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Emma especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Emma has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Emma's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
emma_yin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Emma Yin, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 13 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 13-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Emma's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Emma keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Emma saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Emma plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Emma has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Adhvith Srivatsan has emotionally recovered.

Emma has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Emma has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Emma has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Emma showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17, which means Emma traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Emma entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Emma paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Emma keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Emma has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Emma, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Emma. We will be here, taking notes.

enming_cao.name: Enming Cao
enming_cao.summary:
Representing California, Enming Cao knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 56 entries across 19 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Notably, Enming has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Enming especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries).

Enming has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Linus Yong (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Jaden D'Souza — a trusted BD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Tracy Yu — 2 events in XD, best: #3

A veteran of the junior circuit, Enming has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Enming has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Enming has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
enming_cao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Enming Cao, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Enming has 56 career entries across 19 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Enming saga began at 2022 NW ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Enming's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 15 entries — a stretch where Enming actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Enming is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Enming would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 7 times Enming has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 7 times Enming has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Enming's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Enming plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Enming is 0-for-19. Zero titles in 19 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Enming and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Enming needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Enming has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Enming's texts. The go-to partner is Bella Liu (6 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Zoe Wong has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Enming's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Enming has shown up 9 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Enming has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Enming is not just losing locally — Enming is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cao family travels cross-country so Enming can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Enming has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Enming showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Enming traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Enming entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Enming paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 56 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Enming looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Enming's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Enming has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Enming. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Enya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Enya has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Ethan Li has emotionally recovered.

Enya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Enya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Enya has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Enya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11, which means Enya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Enya entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Enya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Enya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Enya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Enya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Enya. We will be here, taking notes.

erela_liu.name: Erela Liu
erela_liu.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Erela Liu broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 44 entries across 16 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

Erela is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Erela adapts to anyone:
  • Gabby XZ Xiang (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (13 total events in GD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Surya Peechara (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Tanay Kankanala — 2 events in XD, best: #4

Erela has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Erela isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Erela is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
erela_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Erela Liu, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Erela has 44 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Erela saga began at 2023 South CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 Peak Sports South OLC in GD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Erela's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 17 entries — a stretch where Erela actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Erela is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Erela would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Erela has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Erela has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Erela's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Erela plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Erela is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 3rd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Erela and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Erela needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Erela has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Erela's texts. The go-to partner is Gabby XZ Xiang (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Jason Yiheng Luo has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Erela's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Erela has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Erela has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Erela has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Erela showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Erela traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Erela entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Erela paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 44 entries, and 43 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Erela looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Erela's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Erela has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Erela. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

eric_cheng.name: Eric Cheng
eric_cheng.summary:
Hailing from Massachusetts, Eric Cheng has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-3 in XD U19, BD U19, BS U19. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Eric belongs with the best in the country.

Notably, Eric has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Eric competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals top-3 (XD U19, BD U19, BS U19) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Eric has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Lukas Rujia Chang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U19 (12 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)

Eric has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Eric has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Eric brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
eric_cheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eric Cheng, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 37 career entries in 14 tournaments, Eric has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 36 entries where Eric drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 37 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Eric saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Eric experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Eric has reached the finals 2 times — at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), 2023 NE ORC (U17/U19) — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Eric has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eric plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 37 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Eric has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Eric's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Lukas Rujia Chang: 12 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 12 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Lukas Rujia Chang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Erika My Lai has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Eric entered 7 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Eric's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Eric and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Eric 5 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Eric has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Eric is not just losing locally — Eric is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Cheng family travels cross-country so Eric can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eric has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eric showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Eric traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Eric entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Eric paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 37 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Eric. Eric just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Eric has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Eric. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Eric: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The Eric saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Eric has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BS U17, which means Eric traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19), where Eric entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 21st, which is a polite way of saying Eric paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Eric is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Eric wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. eric_tong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eric Tong, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Eric is right on schedule. It all started at 2021 NorCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Eric has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Eric saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eric has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eric showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 NorCal CRC in BS U15, which means Eric traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 NorCal CRC in BD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 NorCal CRC, where Eric entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Eric paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Eric is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Eric wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Eric, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

eric_yu.name: Eric Yu
eric_yu.summary:
Out of California, Eric Yu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U13 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 19 entries in 7 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Eric especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Eric adapts to anyone:
  • Egbert Zhao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Eric has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Eric brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
eric_yu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eric Yu, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 19 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 19-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Eric's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Eric keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Eric saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eric plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 19 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Eric has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Eric's texts. The go-to partner is Egbert Zhao (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Jocelyn Chiang has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eric has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eric showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Eric traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Eric entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Eric paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Eric keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Eric has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Eric, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Eric. We will be here, taking notes.

eric_zhang.name: Eric Zhang
eric_zhang.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Eric Zhang has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 29 entries across 12 tournaments, Eric is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Eric competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Eric adapts to anyone:
  • Sri Raghuram Vasamsetti (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (7 events, best: #4, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Xinyue Jin (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #4)
  • Katie Moy — a trusted XD partner (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Eric has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Eric brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
eric_zhang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eric Zhang, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 29 events across 12 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 29-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Eric's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Eric keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Eric saga began at 2022 Midwest CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eric plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 29 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Eric has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Sri Raghuram Vasamsetti: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Sri Raghuram Vasamsetti has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 Midwest ORC in BD U17 — we hope Sri Raghuram Vasamsetti has emotionally recovered.

Eric has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eric has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eric showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U17, which means Eric traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 Midwest ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Eric entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Eric paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 29 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Eric. Eric just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Eric has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Eric. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Eric: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

eric_zhun_hu.summary:

erica_chen_lin.name: Erica Chen Lin
erica_chen_lin.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Erica Chen Lin wears the gold from Junior Nationals — the single most important tournament for any American junior player in XD U15. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 9 career victories across 22 tournaments, Erica has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Notably, Erica has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Erica especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (5 entries) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U15) • 1 ORC title.

With 17 different doubles partners over the years, Erica adapts to anyone:
  • William Geng (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Jacob Ma (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in Mixed/XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Jade Hu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #3)
  • Jayden Mar (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 6 tournaments. Erica is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Erica has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Erica has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 9 seeded entries across the career, Erica consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 9 titles, Erica is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
erica_chen_lin.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Erica Chen Lin, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. And yes, Erica IS a Junior National Champion — XD U15 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 64-entry career across 22 tournaments with a 14% win rate, which means 55 times — 55! — Erica went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 64 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Erica saga began at 2023 Midwest OLC, where Erica burst onto the scene with a title in GD U13 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Erica has delivered on some of those promises: 9 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Erica promised and what Erica has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 4 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Erica actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 17 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Erica experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Erica has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 Egret MW CRC, where Erica entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, GD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Erica does own 9 actual titles, so it is not like Erica has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 9 wins means Erica is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Erica plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 5 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Erica can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Erica has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Erica's texts. The go-to partner is Pranav Gyajangi (7 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15 — we hope Pranav Gyajangi has emotionally recovered.

Of Erica's 9 career titles, 7 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Erica feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Erica entered 5 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Erica's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Erica and said 'nah.'

Erica has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Erica is not just losing locally — Erica is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 14% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lin family travels cross-country so Erica can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Erica has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Erica showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Erica traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-49 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Erica entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Erica paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 9 titles to the name, 64 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Erica looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Erica's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Erica has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Erica. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

What makes Erika especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Erika adapts to anyone:
  • Isabelle Chen — 2 events in GD, best: #3

Erika has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NW, SoCal, Erika isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Erika brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
erika_my_lai.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Erika My Lai, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 15 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 15-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Erika's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Erika keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Erika saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Erika would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Erika has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Erika has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Erika's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Erika plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Erika has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Eric Cheng has emotionally recovered.

Erika has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Erika has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Erika has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Erika showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17, which means Erika traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Erika entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Erika paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Erika keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Erika has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Erika, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Erika. We will be here, taking notes.

  • Ksheera Karthik — a trusted GD partner (4 events, best: #5)
