With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Esha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
esha_mehta.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Esha Mehta, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 32 events across 12 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 32-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Esha's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Esha keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Esha saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Esha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 32 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Esha has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Alina Aronova: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Alina Aronova has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Aiden S Lau has emotionally recovered.

One thing nobody can accuse Esha of is playing it safe. Esha has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Esha has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Esha has built a permanent residence on that line. Esha has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Esha has 20 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Esha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Esha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Esha entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Esha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 32 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Esha. Esha just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Esha has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Esha. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Esha: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

eshan_neog.name: Eshan Neog
eshan_neog.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Eshan Neog has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Eshan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (6 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Eshan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (11 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 2 ORC titles.

Eshan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 24 different players across the career:
  • Noah Chien (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD
  • Lydia Chao — a trusted U17/XD partner (5 events, best: #4, spanning 4 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 2 tournaments — a clear sign that Eshan is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Eshan has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Eshan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 6 seasons and 7 titles, Eshan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
eshan_neog.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Eshan Neog, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 71 career entries in 27 tournaments, Eshan has 7 titles — a 10% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 9 entries where Eshan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 71 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Eshan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 South CRC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Eshan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 4 titles in 20 entries — a stretch where Eshan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 4 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Eshan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Eshan has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Eshan does own 7 actual titles, so it is not like Eshan has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 7 wins means Eshan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Eshan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Eshan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 24 of them, to be precise. Eshan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Eshan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Savannah H Kam: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Savannah H Kam has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Lydia Chao has emotionally recovered.

Of Eshan's 7 career titles, 5 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Eshan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Eshan entered 11 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Eshan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Eshan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Eshan has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

One thing nobody can accuse Eshan of is playing it safe. Eshan has entered older age groups at 6 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Eshan has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 7 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Eshan has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Eshan is not just losing locally — Eshan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Neog family travels cross-country so Eshan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Eshan has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Eshan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Eshan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Eshan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Eshan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 7 titles to the name, 71 entries, and 64 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Eshan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Eshan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Eshan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Eshan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

With 8 different doubles partners over the years, Esther adapts to anyone:
  • Ella Zhang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (5 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)

  • Harry H Song — 2 events in XD, best: #3

esther_he.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Esther He, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Esther has 26 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Esther saga began at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Esther would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 6 times Esther has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 6 times Esther has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Esther's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Esther plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 26 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Esther has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Esther's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ella Zhang: 5 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 5 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ella Zhang has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U11 — we hope Anirudh Ananth has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Esther has shown up 3 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Esther has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Esther has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Esther showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U11, which means Esther traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Esther entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Esther paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Esther keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Esther has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Esther, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Esther. We will be here, taking notes.

ethan_ao.name: Ethan Ao
ethan_ao.summary:
A Washington competitor, Ethan Ao is a Regional Championship titlist, currently competing at the U19 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Ethan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • 1 ORC title.

With 13 different doubles partners over the years, Ethan adapts to anyone:
  • Alex Anlai Song (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (9 total events in BD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Jocelyn (Youran) Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ethan has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ethan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 3 titles, Ethan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
ethan_ao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Ao, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-5. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ethan has 41 career entries across 17 tournaments and has managed to win 3 of them — a 7% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ethan saga began at 2022 Dave Freeman OLC, where Ethan burst onto the scene with a title in BD U15 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Ethan has delivered on some of those promises: 3 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Ethan promised and what Ethan has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ethan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Ethan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ethan's texts. The go-to partner is Alex Anlai Song (9 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Alex Anlai Song has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Ethan has shown up 6 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ethan is not just losing locally — Ethan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ao family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 41 entries, and 38 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ethan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ethan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ethan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ethan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chan family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. So here we are: 5 seasons, 41 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Ethan. Ethan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ethan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ethan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ethan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ethan is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ethan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 2 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ethan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Ethan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ethan's texts. The go-to partner is Derek Ruoyu Li (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U15 — we hope Trilochan Janakiraman has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ethan's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Ethan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Ethan entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 41st, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 28 entries, and 26 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ethan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ethan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ethan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ethan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ethan_d_li.name: Ethan D Li
ethan_d_li.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Ethan D Li is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U17 level, with 10 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Ethan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Ethan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Ethan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
ethan_d_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan D Li, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Ethan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Ethan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Ethan saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 2 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ethan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Ava Lee has emotionally recovered.

Ethan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ethan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ethan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ethan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ethan. We will be here, taking notes.

ethan_paw.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Paw, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 33 career entries in 12 tournaments, Ethan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 32 entries where Ethan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 33 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ethan saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 33 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ethan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Ben Tirtohadi: 7 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 7 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ben Tirtohadi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17 — we hope Charlotte A Tse has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Ethan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ethan and said 'nah.'

Ethan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ethan is not just losing locally — Ethan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Paw family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in BS U17, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ethan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ethan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ethan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ethan. We will be here, taking notes.

ethan_qiu.name: Ethan Qiu
ethan_qiu.summary:
Representing New Jersey, Ethan Qiu has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U13 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 11 career titles across 34 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Notably, Ethan has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Ethan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 5 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U13 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (XD U11) • 3 ORC titles.

Ethan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 11 different players across the career:
  • Leon Luo (current primary partner) — 13 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (28 total events in BD/U13, spanning 5 seasons)
  • Rachel Yao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (7 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Lilian Feng — 13 events in U13/XD, producing 2 titles together, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 2 titles across 5 tournaments — a clear sign that Ethan is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ethan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ethan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Ethan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 10 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 11 titles, Ethan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ethan_qiu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Qiu, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U15 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Ethan is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Ethan has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Ethan would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Ethan, we kept reading — all 97 entries across 34 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 11% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Ethan held up a trophy, there were roughly 7 other times Ethan walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Ethan saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2022 NE CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ethan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 3 titles in 21 entries — a stretch where Ethan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ethan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ethan has reached 14 championship matches across this career. That is 14 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 14 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NE ORC (U11/U13), where Ethan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (11 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ethan does own 11 actual titles, so it is not like Ethan has never won anything. But 14 finals losses versus 11 wins means Ethan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 5 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ethan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Ethan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ethan's texts. The most successful partnership is with Leon Luo — 28 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Leon Luo deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Leon Luo a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Leon Luo has emotionally recovered.

Of Ethan's 11 career titles, 6 came at local events versus 5 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Ethan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. Then there is the seeding situation. Ethan has been seeded in 29 events and underperformed the seeding 10 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Ethan takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 South OLC in BS U13, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Ethan. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Ethan has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ethan is not just losing locally — Ethan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Qiu family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15), where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 11 titles to the name, 97 entries, and 86 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ethan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ethan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ethan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ethan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ethan_s_paes.name: Ethan S Paes
ethan_s_paes.summary:
A Oregon competitor, Ethan S Paes is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 9 entries across 5 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Ethan competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Ethan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NW, NorCal, Ethan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Ethan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

ethan_sie.name: Ethan Sie
ethan_sie.summary:
A New York competitor, Ethan Sie has stepped onto the competitive stage with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Ethan's.

Ethan competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
ethan_sie.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Sie, from New York, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ethan is right on schedule. Ethan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ethan is not just losing locally — Ethan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 30 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 88 entries, and 82 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ethan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ethan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ethan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ethan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ethan_xiao_ze_yang.name: Ethan Xiao-ze Yang
ethan_xiao_ze_yang.summary:
A California competitor, Ethan Xiao-ze Yang keeps punching through to the quarterfinals, doing so 6 times, currently competing at the U15 level, building a strong foundation across 17 tournaments. Deeper runs are on the horizon.

What makes Ethan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

Ethan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Jonathan Loh (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U13 (16 events, best: #5, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Grace Zichen Qu Zhao (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (4 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Emily Chan (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ethan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ethan has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Ethan brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
ethan_xiao_ze_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Xiao-ze Yang, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 48 career entries in 17 tournaments, Ethan has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 47 entries where Ethan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 48 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Ethan saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 48 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Ethan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ethan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Jonathan Loh: 16 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 16 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jonathan Loh has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Jonathan Loh has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Ethan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Ethan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Ethan has shown up 6 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ethan is not just losing locally — Ethan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 37 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 49th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 48 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Ethan. Ethan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Ethan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Ethan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Ethan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

ethan_xu.name: Ethan Xu
ethan_xu.summary:
Out of California, Ethan Xu is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Ethan's.

Ethan competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).
ethan_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Xu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ethan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 25th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Ethan entered 2 events with results ranging from 9th to 25th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Ethan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Ethan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Ethan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Ethan adapts to anyone:

  • Sizhe Chen — a trusted BD partner (10 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Ethan has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Ethan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

ethan_ys_ao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan YS Ao, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. That means for every win, there are about 49 entries where Ethan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. The Ethan saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ethan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 20 entries — a stretch where Ethan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ethan is 0-for-3. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ethan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ethan has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 NW Oregon OLC, where Ethan entered 3 events and came away with 3 runner-up finishes (BS U15, XD U15, BD U15). 3 chances at gold, 3 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ethan does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Ethan has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 1 wins means Ethan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Ao family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 NW ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ethan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. ethan_yip_ly.name: Ethan Yip Ly
ethan_yip_ly.summary:
Out of California, Ethan Yip Ly has established a commanding presence at the local tournament level, currently competing at the U17 level, racking up 6 titles across 26 tournaments. That winning pedigree at the local and club level creates the perfect launchpad for success at the regional and national stages.

Ethan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #2, 3 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Ethan adapts to anyone:
  • Yian Yang (current primary partner) — 10 events, 1 title together in BD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Olivia Ye (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Bella Liu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)
  • Mizuki Ikeuchi (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Having moved up to U17, Ethan is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Ethan brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ethan has competed across U11, U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Ethan has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Ethan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Ethan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
ethan_yip_ly.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Yip Ly, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Ethan has 74 career entries across 26 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 8% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Ethan saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 17th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal CRC in XD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ethan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 8 entries — a stretch where Ethan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Ethan is 0-for-17. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ethan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ethan has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Ethan does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Ethan has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 6 wins means Ethan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Ethan is 0-for-26. Zero titles in 26 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Ethan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 6 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Ethan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Ethan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Ethan's texts. The go-to partner is Cheree Leung (11 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Cheree Leung has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Ethan's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Ethan has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Tournament committees have handed Ethan a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Ethan has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in BS U11, where Ethan was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Ethan is not just losing locally — Ethan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 8% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ly family travels cross-country so Ethan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 74 entries, and 68 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ethan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ethan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ethan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ethan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

ethan_zheng.name: Ethan Zheng
ethan_zheng.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Ethan Zheng is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U15 level, with 9 entries across 3 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

What makes Ethan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Ethan has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 5 different players across the career:
  • Akshita Tripathi (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Ethan has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.
ethan_zheng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ethan Zheng, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 9 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Ethan is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC, and so far, the only thing Ethan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Ethan saga began at 2023 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ethan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 9 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Ethan has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Akshita Tripathi has emotionally recovered.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Ethan has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Ethan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15, which means Ethan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Ethan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Ethan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Ethan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Ethan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Ethan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Ethan. We will be here, taking notes.
