evan_liu.name: Evan Liu
evan_liu.summary:
Representing Massachusetts, Evan Liu has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U15 BD, U15 XD, U15 BS, U17 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 13 career titles across 28 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Evan is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (9 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Evan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 5 doubles titles, 6 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 BD, U15 XD, U15 BS, U17 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (BS U15) • 10 ORC titles.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Evan adapts to anyone:
  • Sophia Wu (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (9 total events in Mixed/U19/XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Zhenxiang Deng (current partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 4 titles across 5 events in BD, across 2 seasons
  • Justin Xia — 16 events in BD/U15/U17, producing 2 titles together, spanning 3 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 8 podium finishes across 3 tournaments. Evan is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Evan has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17, U19 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Evan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Evan has been seeded #1 or #2 in 12 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 13 titles, Evan is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
evan_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Evan Liu, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Evan is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Evan has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Evan would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Evan, we kept reading — all 79 entries across 28 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 16% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Evan held up a trophy, there were roughly 5 other times Evan walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Evan saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Evan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 6 titles in 23 entries — a stretch where Evan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 8 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Evan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Evan has reached 15 championship matches across this career. That is 15 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 15 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2023 U.S. Selection Event, where Evan entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (U15 BS, U15 BD). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Evan does own 13 actual titles, so it is not like Evan has never won anything. But 15 finals losses versus 13 wins means Evan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Evan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 5 doubles, 6 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Evan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Evan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Evan's texts. The go-to partner is Justin Xia (16 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Cassidy Yeh has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Evan has been seeded in 26 events and underperformed the seeding 11 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Evan takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Evan. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Evan of is playing it safe. Evan has entered older age groups at 9 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Evan has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 12 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Evan has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Evan is not just losing locally — Evan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 16% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Evan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Evan has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Evan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U15, which means Evan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 13 titles to the name, 79 entries, and 66 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Evan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Evan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Evan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Evan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

evan_lu.name: Evan Lu
evan_lu.summary:
Out of California, Evan Lu broke through with a tournament title, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 56 entries across 21 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Evan competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries).

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Evan adapts to anyone:
  • Ellie Chen (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Henry Hongxu Zhang (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #4)

Having moved up to U15, Evan is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Evan brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Evan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Evan has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Evan has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
evan_lu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Evan Lu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 56 career entries in 21 tournaments, Evan has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 55 entries where Evan drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 56 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Evan saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in BD U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Evan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 19 entries — a stretch where Evan actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Evan is 0-for-10. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Evan would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Evan has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Evan has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Evan's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Evan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Evan is 0-for-20. Zero titles in 20 tries. The best singles result is a 5th-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Evan and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Evan needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Evan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Evan's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Siming(Alan) Qu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Siming(Alan) Qu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Aarav Reddy Mukkamala has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Evan's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Evan entered 3 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Evan's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Evan and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Evan has shown up 8 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Evan is not just losing locally — Evan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Lu family travels cross-country so Evan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Evan has 40 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Evan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Evan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Evan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Evan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 56 entries, and 55 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Evan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Evan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Evan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Evan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

evan_mu.summary:
A California competitor, Evan Mu has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U19 level, breaking into the semifinals 1 time. Across 10 entries in 4 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

What makes Evan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Evan has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Anson Chen — 2 events in BD, best: #3

Evan has competed at both U15 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Evan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
evan_mu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Evan Mu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Evan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Evan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Evan saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Evan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Evan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Evan has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Evan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Evan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Evan entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Evan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Evan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Evan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Evan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Evan. We will be here, taking notes.

evan_pathak.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Evan Pathak is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Evan's.

Evan competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
evan_pathak.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Evan Pathak, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Evan is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 South OLC (TBTT), and so far, the only thing Evan has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Evan saga began at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Evan has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Evan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 South OLC (TBTT) in BS U11, which means Evan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Evan is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Evan wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Evan, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

evan_zhou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Evan Zhou, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Evan has 26 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Evan saga began at 2022 NW ORC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Evan has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Evan's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Aiden Liu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Evan has shown up 4 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Evan is not just losing locally — Evan is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhou family travels cross-country so Evan can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Evan has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Evan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Evan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Evan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Evan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 26 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Evan. Evan just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Evan has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Evan. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Evan: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Evelyn has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Evelyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Evelyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 Midwest OLC in GS U13. A pattern, not a blip.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 17 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Evelyn. Evelyn just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Evelyn has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Evelyn. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Evelyn: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

evelyn_xiaoran_ge.name: Evelyn Xiaoran Ge
evelyn_xiaoran_ge.summary:
Representing Minnesota, Evelyn Xiaoran Ge is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U17 level, with 10 entries across 4 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Evelyn competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Evelyn has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Rheya Huang (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

Evelyn has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Evelyn's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
evelyn_xiaoran_ge.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Evelyn Xiaoran Ge, from Minnesota, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Evelyn's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Evelyn keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Evelyn saga began at 2024 Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Evelyn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Evelyn has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Evelyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Evelyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Evelyn entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Evelyn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Evelyn keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Evelyn has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Evelyn, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Evelyn. We will be here, taking notes.
